query: carbohydrate consumption pattern and VLDL levels

retrieved_docs.title: Behavior Modification for Lifestyle Improvement -- Issues of Concern -- Diet Quality
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding health-compromising dietary behaviors, studies have shown an association between high saturated-fat diets and increased levels of low-density lipoproteins (LDL) and very low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), linked to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. [15] [16] Nutritional guidelines emphasize that adults in the United States consume too many calories, saturated fats, sodium, and added sugars and don't consume enough fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypertriglyceridemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The first step in the management of FHTG is lifestyle modification. Patients usually benefit from a low-fat diet, reducing intake of simple carbohydrates and sugars, and avoiding alcohol. Although dietary fat is not a primary source of serum triglycerides, consuming primarily monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats rather than saturated fats may improve the lipid profile. Depending on triglyceride levels, recommendations include a total fat intake of 10% to 30% of total calories. Eating fatty fish or taking fish oil supplements with omega-3 fatty acids may also help reduce VLDL and triglyceride levels. [28] [29] [30] Dietary recommendations include consuming high-fiber foods such as whole grains, legumes, fruits, and vegetables. In addition, clinicians should encourage patients to participate in regular, moderate, and high-intensity physical activities to improve triglyceride levels. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Diet, sex, and race are factors that significantly influence VLDL production and secretion. VLDL synthesis is mainly related to lipid intake, with secretion increasing in the postprandial state. High-fat diets have been associated with higher VLDL secretion, usually presenting as increased blood triglyceride levels. [21] [22] Studies have inconsistently reported the timing of postprandial VLDL peaks, ranging from 30 minutes up to 5 or 6 hours. Nonetheless, multiple studies have found that insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, and obesity are strongly associated with longer plateaus and higher peaks of VLDL levels. [23] [24] Women have also been shown to have lower VLDL concentrations than men, although the cause of this sex difference is not completely understood. [25] VLDL has been associated with the development of multiple pathological conditions involving the cardiovascular, endocrine, renal, hepatic, and nervous systems, as well as cancer and several autoimmune and dermatological diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Carbohydrate Diet -- Issues of Concern -- Lipid Response
retrieved_docs.content: Incorporating more fat and protein in response to reducing dietary carbs has led to concerns about the effect of low-carb dieting on lipids, specifically LDL cholesterol. Recent systematic reviews of low-carb diets on lipids demonstrate a neutral-to-small increase in LDL, a favorable triglycerides reduction, and an increase in HDL cholesterol, particularly in those assigned to the very–low-carb intervention. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Abetalipoproteinemia -- Evaluation -- Fasting Lipid Profile
retrieved_docs.content: Fasting lipid profile shows low VLDLs, LDLs, and total cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fasting -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Another test that traditionally requires a patient to be fasting for accuracy includes triglyceride measurement on a lipid panel. Blood triglycerides are present in substantial quantity in the carrier proteins chylomicrons and very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL). Chylomicrons are responsible for carrying triglycerides from digested food to peripheral tissues while VLDL is made in the liver and represents a baseline blood triglyceride level resilient to food intake. Therefore, an accurate measurement of blood triglycerides in VLDL requires a patient to be fasting to exclude chylomicron triglycerides from the measurement. Recent data suggest that accurate lipid measurement may be possible in the absence of fasting although fasting for lipid panels is still recommended by most national and international guidelines. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Low Fat Diet -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have also raised concern over the potential of lowering HDL cholesterol, raising triglycerides, and cause unfavorable postprandial lipemic changes. [7] [8] So much so that the “2013 AHA/ACC Guideline on Lifestyle Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk” did not state any recommendations for dietary cholesterol and indicated a lack of sufficient evidence to show that lowering of dietary cholesterol reduces LDL-C (low-density lipoprotein cholesterol) or not. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The average primary care clinician's median visit length is only 10 minutes, which often limits discussions on the root cause of low HDL levels. [37] Many members of the health care team can assist in counseling patients and supporting them over multiple visits to optimize lifestyle modifications. Moreover, licensed therapists can assist with behavioral modification strategies, including smoking and alcohol cessation, and improved eating habits. Dieticians are also beneficial for individual consultations and for teaching patient classes about healthier cooking habits.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoalphalipoproteinemia -- Treatment / Management -- Lifestyle Changes
retrieved_docs.content: Smoking cessation may yield an increase in HDL levels in thirty days. [35] Weight loss, either through dieting or exercise, causes a favorable change in plasma lipoprotein levels. [36] The combination of frequent exercise and diet will improve plasma lipoprotein levels even more. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Carbohydrate Diet -- Clinical Significance -- Cardiovascular Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned above, the effect of low-carb diets on cardiovascular risk factors continues to be controversial. While a few studies have demonstrated an increase in LDL cholesterol with low-carb diets, others showed negligible changes. However, other metabolic markers, such as lowering triglycerides and increases in HDL, have been demonstrated with low-carb diets. [4] [27] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertriglyceridemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Lifestyle changes include dietary changes such as reduction of carbohydrate intake, avoidance of sugar-sweetened beverages, and processed carbohydrates, regular exercise, and weight loss. Weight loss of 5% to 10% is associated with a reduction of triglyceride levels. Similarly, regular aerobic exercise can reduce triglycerides. The amount of decrease in triglycerides due to exercise and dietary modifications is usually variable. [15] Many studies have shown that monounsaturated fatty acid-rich Mediterranean-type diet has reduced postprandial lipemia. When triglyceride levels are in excess of 500 mg/dL, restriction of dietary fat is key to avoid a postprandial rise in chylomicrons and thereby reduce the risk of pancreatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Carbohydrates -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals who eat high fiber diets also tend to have low serum cholesterol and high HDL levels than people who consume a low fiber diet; the lowering of cholesterol also decreases the risk of heart disease. In many parts of Africa, people who eat high fiber diets tend to have a very low risk of intestinal cancer. But the exact amount of fiber to eat to prevent colon cancer remains unknown. Anecdotal reports maintain that eating fiber may lower blood pressure, lower the incidence of gallstones, and decrease blood sugar.

retrieved_docs.title: Low Fat Diet -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: There have been multiple issues of concern and controversies around the concept of a low-fat diet. The biggest concern with the promotion of the low-fat diet has been that manufacturing companies are touting products labeled as low-fat products, where they are replacing the fat with large amounts of refined carbohydrates, which increase the risk of metabolic disorders and hypertriglyceridemia. Studies are also reporting that diets rich in carbohydrates, and low in unsaturated fat, can also negatively impact lipoprotein risk factors and increase cardiovascular risks. [3] There is also a proposed theory that refined carbohydrates decrease the cardioprotective action of HDL by altering its metabolic functions. [4] There has undoubtedly been a focus on replacing the carbohydrates for fats, but the specificities of the replaced carbohydrates remain poorly defined. [5] These concerns have led to the development of alternative dietary approaches. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Tangier Disease -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: To maintain a healthy lifestyle and to keep lipids profile within the regular limit there are many advises to be given include: regular aerobic exercise, maintaining a healthy weight, smoking cessation, and replacement of monounsaturated for saturated fatty acids raise HDL cholesterol. There are also certain types of food which are recommended for patients that are at risk for a CVS events that are rich in omega-3 fatty acids and could increase the HDL level and lower the LDL level includes; Olive oil, whole grains, beans and legumes, High fiber fruits, fatty fish, Flax and chia seeds, nuts and avocado. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Carbohydrate Diet -- Issues of Concern -- Lipid Response
retrieved_docs.content: Research recently indicated that a subset of lean individuals, called lean mass hyper-responders, might have a hyper LDL response with ketogenic diets. [18] Due to the varied and individualized response, recommendations are for baseline fasting lipid profile, periodic testing, and shared decision-making.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Testing -- Lipids
retrieved_docs.content: To test the amount of lipids in the body, a sample of blood is taken via venipuncture in tubes and analyzed for serum or plasma total cholesterol, triglycerides, and lipoproteins. Cholesterol levels remain fairly constant so that sampling is possible at any time. In contrast to cholesterol levels, triglyceride levels fluctuate throughout the day, so blood collection should occur after a 12-hour fasting period because, at that point, chylomicrons should have cleared from the circulation. To separate and quantify lipoproteins from the plasma sample, ultracentrifugation, precipitation, and electrophoresis are performed. High vs. low plasma lipid levels are determined based on the bell-shaped distribution of the general population. Cholesterol levels are determined based on the risk for coronary atherosclerosis. Cholesterol levels below 200 mg/dl are considered desirable, levels from 200 to 239 mg/dl are considered borderline high cholesterol, and levels of 240 mg/dl or greater are considered high cholesterol. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering medication, produces a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, reflecting its influence on the breakdown of triglycerides and overall lipid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2741
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that are used to improve abnormal blood lipid profiles, particularly by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol. The primary members of this plain fibrate class are bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. All three agents act as agonists of peroxisome proliferator‑activated receptor‑α (PPAR‑α), leading to increased oxidation of fatty acids, reduced hepatic production of very‑low‑density lipoprotein, and enhanced clearance of triglyceride‑rich lipoproteins. Because of these mechanisms, bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát are employed in the management of hypertriglyceridaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and certain cases of secondary dyslipidaemia, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction when used appropriately in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic diseases that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. In addition to lipid abnormalities, this category is linked to elevated blood glucose conditions such as new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)), highlighting the close interplay between lipid and glucose metabolic pathways. Recognizing that hyperlipoproteinemia and early diabetes can coexist within the spectrum of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores the importance of integrated assessment and management of both lipid and glucose abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: Bempedoic acid is a pharmacologic agent that is not classified as a hypoglycemic drug, indicating it does not lower blood glucose levels. Instead, bempedoic acid may be employed in the treatment of type II hyperlipidemia, a lipid disorder characterized by elevated cholesterol and triglyceride levels. This distinction highlights bempedoic acid’s role in managing dyslipidemia rather than glycemic control, which is important for clinicians selecting appropriate therapies for patients with abnormal lipid profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1941
retrieved_docs.content: Above reference range is used as an interpretation when evaluating disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that laboratory values exceeding the normal limits may reflect underlying lipid metabolic abnormalities. Within the category of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific subtype, representing conditions characterized by excessive circulating lipoproteins. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset prediabetes) is considered a member of the broader disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical interrelationship between dysglycemia and lipid metabolic disturbances. Understanding these connections assists clinicians in interpreting abnormal lipid panels, identifying hyperlipoproteinemic states, and recognizing the metabolic overlap with early glucose dysregulation for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that encompass several specific entities. Within this category, اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) are recognized as subtypes, indicating that these conditions are forms of lipid metabolism disorders. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early diabetes. The condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, emphasizing the genetic basis of this specific lipid disorder. Furthermore, اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات (carbohydrate metabolism disorder) is identified as having اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي as its parent, linking carbohydrate metabolic abnormalities to the broader metabolic disorder framework. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid and carbohydrate metabolic disorders are interrelated, how specific lipid abnormalities can be genetically inherited, and how they may present clinically alongside early diabetic changes, informing a comprehensive approach to patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analog, exerts its pharmacologic action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis. Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering agent, also leads to decreased lipolysis as a physiologic effect. Thus, both insulin aspart and klofibrát share the outcome of reduced lipolysis, although insulin aspart achieves this through direct activation of the INSR protein, while klofibrát acts via its own distinct metabolic pathway. Recognizing this common effect can inform therapeutic decisions when managing conditions related to lipid metabolism and glucose regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate and fenofibrate are both classified as fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As members of the fibrate class, these agents are used to alter lipid metabolism, typically to lower triglyceride levels and raise high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol, thereby contributing to the management of dyslipidaemia. Their shared classification underscores their similar pharmacologic actions and therapeutic role in cardiovascular risk reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. This relationship underscores the importance of simultaneous screening for lipid abnormalities in patients with type 2 diabetes and integrated management of both cholesterol and glycemic control to improve overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrát is classified as a member of the “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain” group, indicating that it belongs to the fibrate class of plain lipid‑modifying agents. As a fibrate, fenofibrát shares the typical pharmacologic actions of this drug family, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën, a broad classification of metabolic disorders affecting lipoproteins and other lipid abnormalities, includes the specific condition فرط ليبيدات الدم, which denotes hyperlipidemia characterized by elevated blood lipid levels. Recognizing فرط ليبيدات الدم as a child entity within this larger category helps clinicians organize lipid disorders and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
